result:
# The Weight of Small Kindnesses

I saw a man help another man tie his shoe yesterday. 

They were both old—the kind of old where bones remember every winter and movement becomes negotiation. We were waiting at a crosswalk, that peculiar urban limbo where strangers stand closer than they'd ever choose to otherwise. The first man, wearing a cardigan despite the heat, noticed the second man staring down at his untied lace with an expression of pure calculation. How to bend. Whether to bend. What the cost would be.

Without a word, the man in the cardigan knelt down—slowly, carefully, like a ceremony—and tied the other man's shoe. The second man said nothing. The first man said nothing. The light changed. We all crossed.

I've been thinking about that moment for three days now.

Not because it was extraordinary. Maybe that's exactly why—because it wasn't. Because small kindnesses happen constantly, unremarked upon, unremembered. They're the dark matter of human existence, invisible but everywhere, holding things together in ways we can't quite measure.

My grandmother used to say that God keeps track of every sparrow that falls, and I always thought that was supposed to be comforting, but honestly it just sounded exhausting. Imagine the bookkeeping. But maybe that's the point—that the small things matter precisely because they're small, because they're countless, because they happen in the margins where we think nobody's watching.

I'm not religious, but I've started keeping my own accounting.

The barista who remembers I drink oat milk and has it ready before I ask. My neighbor who brings in my packages when it rains. The stranger on the train who pretended not to notice I was crying. The teenager who held the door for me and my impossible grocery bags, then blushed red when I thanked him, as if kindness were something to be embarrassed about.

Is kindness becoming countercultural? 

We live in an age of optimization. Efficiency. Self-care as a commandment. I see people—mostly people my age, mostly online—talking about boundaries like they're building fortresses. And yes, boundaries are important. Protection is important. But somewhere between martyrdom and self-preservation, there's supposed to be a place where we still tie each other's shoes.

I'm guilty of it too. I've walked past homeless people with my headphones in, creating a bubble of deliberate disconnection. I've ignored emails asking for small favors because I was "protecting my energy." I've calculated the cost of generosity like a accountant at tax season, trying to determine if I could afford to care.

When did caring become something we have to afford?

My therapist says I'm too hard on myself. That awareness is the first step. That I can't pour from an empty cup, and other platitudes that are true but incomplete. Because here's what nobody tells you: the cup fills both ways. Sometimes you pour out and somehow there's more, not less.

The shoe-tying man didn't look depleted when he stood back up. He looked fine. He looked like someone who'd done something that cost him almost nothing and gave someone else almost everything.

That's the exchange rate that breaks economics.

I used to work with a woman who would leave sticky notes on people's desks. Not for any reason. Just things like "You're doing great" or "Someone's glad you exist." It was objectively cheesy. We made fun of her for it, gently, in the way you make fun of someone for being too good. She left the company two years ago and people still talk about those notes. I still have mine in a drawer somewhere.

What are we saving ourselves for, exactly?

I don't mean we should all become saints or sacrifice ourselves on the altar of everyone else's needs. I just mean—maybe the calculations are wrong. Maybe we're measuring in the wrong currency. Maybe the man in the cardigan understood something about abundance that the rest of us missed while we were busy protecting our scarcity.

The internet has made us fluent in cruelty and suspicious of gentleness. Kindness gets called performative, naive, a luxury for people who don't understand how hard the world really is. And yes, the world is hard. The world is unfathomably hard. Which is exactly why the soft things matter.

I'm trying something new. Small kindnesses, deliberately. Not to be virtuous or collect karma points, but because I'm curious. Because that man at the crosswalk did something to me, rearranged something, and I want to know if it replicates.

So far: I've paid for the coffee of the person behind me in line. I've complimented a stranger's tattoo. I've let someone merge in traffic. I've texted three people to tell them I was thinking about them. I've left a bigger tip. I've smiled at a crying child instead of looking annoyed.

Nothing has exploded. The world hasn't transformed. I'm not enlightened.

But something feels different. Lighter, maybe. Like I've been holding my breath for years and just now remembered I could exhale.

The man whose shoe was tied—I wonder if he thinks about it. Probably not. Probably it was just Tuesday, just another crosswalk, just another stranger. Or maybe he went home and called his daughter. Maybe he felt less alone. Maybe it gave him permission to ask for help the next time he needs it.

We'll never know the full weight of our small kindnesses. They ripple out in directions we can't track, touching lives in ways we'll never hear about. That used to frustrate me—the not knowing. Now I think it might be the whole point.

We tie each other's shoes.

We do it without contracts or guarantees or return policies. We do it because someone's shoe is untied and we have hands that work and thirty seconds we'll never miss.

The light changes.

We all cross.
